revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ame-no-ukihashi]][[it:Ame-no-ukihashi]][[ja:天浮橋]]
'''Ame-no-ukihashi''' (天浮橋,<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Picken|first1=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yA3_11C6pPgC&dq=Ame-no-Ukihashi&pg=PA356|title=Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings|date=1994|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26431-3|page=356|qq=}}</ref> 天の浮橋;<ref>{{Cite book|last=Goto|first=Akira|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rBEHEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA25|title=Cultural Astronomy of the Japanese Archipelago: Exploring the Japanese Skyscape|date=2020-11-19|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-000-22109-1|page=25|qq=}}</ref> English: ''Floating Bridge of Heaven'') is the bridge that connects the heaven and the earth in [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA4|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|page=4|qq=}}</ref> In the story of the [[Kuniumi|creation of the Japanese archipelago]], narrated in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the gods [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} stood upon this bridge while they gave form to the world.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&pg=PA38|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|page=38|qq=}}</ref> The concept of the Floating Bridge may have been inspired by the {{ill|Rainbow|en|Rainbow|qid=Q1052|lt=Rainbow|1=Rainbow}},<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Lee |first1=Raymond L. |last2=Fraser |first2=Alistair B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kZcCtT1ZeaEC&pg=PA31 |title=The Rainbow Bridge: Rainbows in Art, Myth, and Science |date=2001 |publisher=Pennsylvania State University Press |isbn=978-0-271-01977-2 |pages=31–32|qq=}}</ref> although it has also been suggested that it represents the {{ill|Milky Way|en|Milky Way|qid=Q321|lt=Milky Way|1=Milky Way}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA161 |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |date=2003 |publisher=ABC-CLIO |isbn=978-1-57607-467-1|qq=}}</ref> In another story, the bridge is said to be guarded by the god [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]], who refuses to allow [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]] to descend to earth. [[Ame-no-Uzume]] persuades Sarutahiko to relent, and subsequently marries him.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&pg=PA321 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |page=321|qq=}}</ref> According to the ''Tango [[Fudoki]]'', the floating bridge eventually collapsed and fell to earth, with its remains forming the area west of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.<ref name=":0" /> However, the 19th-century {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=nativist|1=Kokugaku}} {{ill|Hayashi Ōen|en|Hayashi Ōen|qid=Q5686042|lt=Hayashi Ōen|1=Hayashi Ōen}} believed the bridge had survived into modern times but was ordinarily both invisible and intangible to those who had not purified their hearts of foreign cultural influence.<ref name=Ishihara>{{cite book |last1=Ishihara 石原 |first1=Shiko'o 醜男 |title=神風連血涙史 Shinpūren Ketsuruishi |date=April 13, 1935 |publisher=大日社 Dainichisha |edition=1st|qq=}}</ref> == References == {{reflist|qq=}}
[[en:Ame-no-ukihashi]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:天浮橋]] {{wikidata link|Q108376881}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:橋の文化]]
